section.title: The Way of Love
section.sectionRationale: This is the most excellent way Paul promised at the end of chapter 12, set between the teaching on gifts and its application to worship. It is not a detour into poetry but the beating heart of the argument: gifts without love are worthless, love is the character that must govern their use, and love alone endures when every gift has passed away. The chapter exposes the emptiness of Corinthian pride and holds up the very character of Christ.
section.thesis: Without love the most impressive gifts are nothing, for love is patient and kind and self-giving and never fails, while prophecy, tongues, and knowledge are partial and will pass away; so faith, hope, and love remain, and the greatest of these is love.
section.frameNote: Three movements: without love the greatest gifts are nothing; the portrait of what love is and is not; and love that never fails, outlasting the partial gifts, so that of faith, hope, and love the greatest is love.

verses.text: If I speak in the tongues of men and of angels, but have not {{agape|love}}, I am only a ringing gong or a clanging cymbal.
If I have the gift of prophecy and can fathom all mysteries and all knowledge, and if I have absolute faith so as to move mountains, but have not love, I am nothing.
If I give all I possess to the poor and exult in the surrender of my body, but have not love, I gain nothing.
Love is patient, love is kind. It does not envy, it does not boast, it is not proud.
It is not rude, it is not self-seeking, it is not easily angered, it keeps no account of wrongs.
Love takes no pleasure in evil, but rejoices in the truth.
It bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things.

units.label: Love never fails
units.note: the greatest of these is love

verses.text: Love never fails. But where there are prophecies, they will cease; where there are tongues, they will be restrained; where there is knowledge, it will be dismissed.
For we know in part and we prophesy in part,
but when the {{teleios|perfect}} comes, the partial passes away.
When I was a child, I talked like a child, I thought like a child, I reasoned like a child. When I became a man, I set aside childish ways.
Now we see but a dim reflection as in a mirror; then we shall see face to face. Now I know in part; then I shall know fully, even as I am fully known.
And now these three remain: {{pistis|faith}}, {{elpis|hope}}, and love; but the greatest of these is love.

agape.gloss: Self-giving, sacrificial love that seeks the good of another regardless of merit. It is not mere feeling or affection but a settled, active commitment, the love God has shown in Christ. This is the love that must govern every gift; without it the most spectacular endowment is nothing, and this love alone never fails.

teleios.gloss: Complete, mature, perfect, that which has reached its goal. When the perfect comes, the partial passes away. Read together with seeing face to face in verse 12, it points most naturally to the completeness we will know at Christ's return, when partial knowledge gives way to full sight, though its exact reference is debated.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, and reliance upon God and His promises. One of the three that remain, faith lays hold of what God has done and said. Yet even faith will one day give way to sight, whereas love endures forever, which is why Paul names love the greatest of the three.

elpis.gloss: Hope, the confident expectation of the good God has promised. Hope reaches toward what is not yet seen and sustains the believer through the present age. Like faith it belongs to this life of waiting, but love outlasts even hope's fulfillment, remaining forever the greatest.

basic.context: Between his teaching on spiritual gifts in chapter 12 and its application to worship in chapter 14, Paul unveils the most excellent way. The Corinthians prized gifts as marks of status; Paul insists that any gift, however dazzling, is worthless without love. He then paints love's character in a series of plain descriptions, and shows that while the gifts they treasured are partial and passing, love never fails. Of the three great abiding realities, faith, hope, and love, the greatest is love.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter is not a stand-alone poem but part of a sustained argument; love is the answer to the pride and division that spoiled their use of gifts. The portrait in verses 4 to 7 is deliberately convicting, describing the very virtues Corinth lacked. Verses 8 to 13 set the temporary against the permanent to reorder their priorities around what will last.
basic.originalLanguages: The key word is agape, self-giving love that seeks another's good, the love of God in Christ, not mere feeling. Paul says the gifts are partial until the teleios, the perfect or complete, comes, when we will see face to face. And of the three that remain, pistis, faith, and elpis, hope, belong to this life of waiting, but love outlasts them both, and so is the greatest.
basic.typology: The love described here is the character of God Himself, supremely displayed at the cross. Every line of verses 4 to 7 is fulfilled in Jesus: patient and kind, not self-seeking, bearing and enduring all things for us. The chapter is a portrait of Christ, and the pattern of the love His people are called and empowered to show.
basic.christ: Read the description of love and you are reading a description of Jesus. He is patient and kind, keeps no record of wrongs, bears all things and endures all things, and gives Himself entirely for those who did not deserve it. The love that never fails is the love that took Him to the cross. He is both the perfect embodiment of this love and the source from which His people learn to love.
basic.god: God is love, and this chapter shows what that love is like: not sentiment but sacrificial, patient, truthful, enduring commitment. The love commanded here is first the love God has for us and pours into us by His Spirit. Because love is what God is, love is what will remain when this age and its partial gifts have passed away.
basic.crossReferences: 1 John 4 says God is love and that we love because He first loved us. Romans 5 tells how God's love is poured into our hearts by the Spirit. John 13 records Jesus' command to love as He has loved. Galatians 5 lists love as the first fruit of the Spirit.
basic.oneStory: From beginning to end the Bible's story is the story of God's love pursuing a wayward people, reaching its climax at the cross and its consummation when we see Him face to face. Gifts belong to this age of partial knowing; love belongs to God's eternal nature and so endures forever. To walk in love is to live now by the reality that will fill the age to come.

context.literary: The centerpiece of chapters 12 to 14, framed by the promise of the most excellent way at 12:31 and the call to pursue love at 14:1. It unfolds in three movements: the necessity of love, verses 1 to 3; the nature of love, verses 4 to 7; and the permanence of love, verses 8 to 13. Its elevated, rhythmic style has made it beloved, but its force is argumentative, correcting the misuse of gifts.
context.historical: Corinth prized eloquence, knowledge, and ecstatic tongues as signs of spiritual attainment and social honor. Paul's portrait of love directly indicts their pride, envy, rudeness, and self-seeking, the very faults visible throughout the letter, and redefines true spirituality as love rather than the possession of impressive gifts.
context.canonical: The chapter gathers the Bible's teaching that love fulfills the law, Romans 13, and that God Himself is love, 1 John 4. It stands with Jesus' summary of the commandments and His new commandment to love one another, presenting love as the abiding mark of the people of God and the character of God revealed in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that love is indispensable, that it is a concrete, sacrificial character rather than a feeling, and that it alone endures, so that the Corinthians will pursue love above the gifts they prize and use every gift in its service.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The hymn describes what love is and does, but it functions as a command: this is the way believers must walk. It does not disparage the gifts, which chapter 14 urges them to pursue, but subordinates them to love, since gifts are for this age while love is forever.

debates.question: What is the perfect that comes in verse 10, when the partial gifts pass away?

views.label: The return of Christ and the age to come
views.case: Verse 12 defines the moment as seeing face to face and knowing fully, language of the final vision of God; the perfect is the completeness we will know when Christ returns, so the gifts belong to this present age.

views.label: The completion of God's written revelation
views.case: Some hold that the perfect is the completed canon, so that the revelatory gifts of prophecy, tongues, and knowledge ceased once Scripture was complete and the church was established on the apostolic foundation.

debates.bearing: On the first view the passage leaves open whether such gifts continue until Christ returns; on the second it teaches their cessation. Either way the chapter's main point stands: love outlasts every gift, so love must be supreme now.

study.originalLanguages: The governing word is agape, the self-giving love that seeks another's good regardless of merit, the very love of God shown in Christ, not warm feeling or natural affection. Paul contrasts the partial gifts with the teleios, the perfect or complete, that is coming, tied in verse 12 to seeing face to face and knowing fully. And he names three that remain: pistis, faith, and elpis, hope, both of which belong to this life of trust and waiting, alongside love which outlasts them both. Because faith will become sight and hope will be fulfilled, but love simply endures, love is the greatest.

typology.type: Love as the character of God
typology.fulfillment: The portrait of love in verses 4 to 7 is fulfilled and embodied in Jesus Christ, who is patient, kind, self-giving, and enduring, supremely at the cross.
Love is the fulfillment of the law and the prophets, so this chapter shows the goal of God's commands realized in the love that Christ shows and the Spirit works in His people.

study.christ: To read this chapter is to read a portrait of Jesus. He is patient and kind; He does not envy or boast; He is not self-seeking or easily angered; He keeps no record of wrongs; He bears, believes, hopes, and endures all things. The love that never fails is the love that carried Him to the cross for people who did not deserve it. Christ is both the perfect embodiment of this love and its source: only as His people are united to Him and filled with His Spirit can they love like this. The most excellent way is finally the way of the crucified and risen Lord.
study.god: The chapter unfolds what it means that God is love. His love is not sentiment but sacrificial, patient, truthful, and enduring commitment to the good of others. The love commanded here is first the love God has shown us and pours into our hearts by His Spirit, so that our loving is a response to and a reflection of His. Because love is intrinsic to who God is, love is the one thing that will remain in full when this age with its partial gifts has passed away.

crossReferences.note: God is love, and we love because He first loved us, showing the source of the love Paul describes.
God's love is poured into our hearts through the Holy Spirit given to us, the power to love this way.
Jesus commands His disciples to love one another as He has loved them, the mark of true discipleship.
Love heads the fruit of the Spirit, showing this love as the Spirit's work, not mere human effort.
Love is the fulfillment of the law, matching love as the most excellent way.

study.oneStory: The whole Bible tells of God's love pursuing a wayward people, love that reaches its climax at the cross and its consummation when His people see Him face to face. The spiritual gifts belong to this age of partial knowing and will pass away; love belongs to God's eternal being and endures forever. To walk in love now is to live already by the reality that will fill the coming age, when faith becomes sight, hope is fulfilled, and love remains.

quiz.q: What does Paul say about the greatest gifts exercised without love?

options.text: They are nothing, and the person gains nothing.
options.note: Verses 2 and 3: without love, I am nothing and gain nothing.

options.text: They are still useful but slightly diminished.
options.note: Paul is far stronger; without love they amount to nothing.

options.text: They are acceptable as long as they help the church.
options.note: Even prophecy and self-sacrifice count for nothing without love.

options.text: They earn a reward regardless of the heart.
options.note: Without love there is no gain, whatever the outward act.

quiz.q: Which of these best captures how Paul describes love in verses 4 to 7?

options.text: Patient, kind, not self-seeking, keeping no account of wrongs, bearing and enduring all things.
options.note: This is the portrait Paul paints of love's character.

options.text: A strong feeling of affection toward those we like.
options.note: Biblical love is active and self-giving, not mainly a feeling.

options.text: Tolerance that never speaks of right or wrong.
options.note: Love rejoices in the truth; it is not indifferent to evil.

options.text: Getting what you deserve from others.
options.note: Love is not self-seeking; it seeks the good of others.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean when he says love never fails?

options.text: Love endures forever, while prophecy, tongues, and knowledge are partial and will pass away.
options.note: Verse 8: the gifts cease, but love remains.

options.text: That loving people will always get the response they hope for.
options.note: The point is love's permanence, not guaranteed returns from others.

options.text: That our efforts to love are always successful.
options.note: It speaks of love's enduring nature, not our flawless performance.

options.text: That love will eventually be replaced by knowledge.
options.note: The opposite: knowledge passes away, love endures.

quiz.q: What does the contrast between now and then in verse 12 describe?

options.text: Now we see dimly and know in part; then we shall see face to face and know fully.
options.note: Verse 12 contrasts present partial knowing with future full sight.

options.text: That we already know everything there is to know of God.
options.note: Paul says we now know only in part; fullness is still future.

options.text: That knowledge of God is impossible in this life.
options.note: We do know truly, but partially; the fullness comes later.

options.text: That God will never be fully known even in glory.
options.note: Then I shall know fully, even as I am fully known.

quiz.q: Of faith, hope, and love, which is the greatest, and why?

options.text: Love, because it endures forever while faith and hope belong to this life of waiting.
options.note: Verse 13 names love the greatest of the three that remain.

options.text: Faith, because it is how we are first saved.
options.note: Faith is vital, but Paul names love as the greatest here.

options.text: Hope, because it looks to the future.
options.note: Hope endures for now, but love is called the greatest.

options.text: They are exactly equal in every respect.
options.note: Paul explicitly ranks love as the greatest of the three.

questions: Paul says even the most impressive gifts are nothing without love. Where are you tempted to measure your spiritual life by ability or achievement rather than by love?
Read verses 4 to 7 slowly, putting your own name in place of the word love. Which lines expose where you most need to grow?
Love keeps no account of wrongs. Is there a record of grievances you are holding against someone that love would call you to release?
The gifts you value most belong to this passing age, but love endures forever. How might that reorder what you give your energy to this week?
Every line describing love is true of Jesus toward you. How does receiving His patient, self-giving love free you to love others that way?

translationNotes.note: Love throughout the chapter translates agape, self-giving love that seeks another's good; it is not mere affection or feeling but active, sacrificial commitment, the love God shows in Christ.

The perfect renders teleios, the complete or mature; in light of seeing face to face in verse 12 it most naturally points to the completeness of the age to come, though some take it as the completed revelation of Scripture.
A dim reflection as in a mirror reflects the polished bronze mirrors of the ancient world, which gave a real but imperfect image; the contrast is present partial knowledge versus future full sight.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 13:1-13; any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas, with wording unchanged.
